coredata.dc:title: Advanced functional materials as high-performance fluorometric sensors for explosives detection

coredata.dc:description: The detection and identification of high-energy materials are critical for public safety, national security, and environmental protection. Despite advances, detecting trace explosives remains challenging due to the lack of low-cost, sensitive, and selective sensors. Fluorescence quenching, a process based on the interaction between fluorophores and quenchers, has emerged as a promising method for explosive sensing. This review examines a wide range of functional materials employed in fluorescent sensors, including molecular assemblies, quantum dots, metal and polymer based nanocomposites, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs), and coordination polymers. These materials generally exhibit fluorescence quenching when exposed to nitroaromatic compounds, making them suitable candidates for explosive detection. The review also discusses the synthesis methods, morphological characteristics, and fluorescence mechanisms of these materials, alongside their performance in detection applications. By compiling the latest advancements in this field, this review provides a comprehensive overview of fluorescence quenching-based sensors, offering a valuable resource for future developments in robust and efficient explosive detection technologies.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection and identification of high-energy materials are critical for public safety, national security, and environmental protection. Despite advances, detecting trace explosives remains challenging due to the lack of low-cost, sensitive, and selective sensors. Fluorescence quenching, a process based on the interaction between fluorophores and quenchers, has emerged as a promising method for explosive sensing. This review examines a wide range of functional materials employed in fluorescent sensors, including molecular assemblies, quantum dots, metal and polymer based nanocomposites, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs), and coordination polymers. These materials generally exhibit fluorescence quenching when exposed to nitroaromatic compounds, making them suitable candidates for explosive detection. The review also discusses the synthesis methods, morphological characteristics, and fluorescence mechanisms of these materials, alongside their performance in detection applications. By compiling the latest advancements in this field, this review provides a comprehensive overview of fluorescence quenching-based sensors, offering a valuable resource for future developments in robust and efficient explosive detection technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Explosives are volatile substances that have the potential to rapidly release energy in the form of a violent explosion. They have been integral to both industrial and military applications for centuries. These materials are utilized in construction, mining, demolition, and pyrotechnics, playing a crucial role in shaping modern society. However, their destructive power and potential for misuse underscores the critical importance of developing reliable and advanced methods for their detection. The detection of explosives has become a paramount concern in today's security landscape [1,2]. The proliferation of terrorism and criminal activities involving explosive devices has necessitated the development of cutting-edge technologies capable of identifying hidden explosives in various settings. From airports and transportation hubs to public spaces and sensitive installations, the ability to accurately and efficiently detect explosives is vital to safeguarding lives, infrastructure, and public order [3,4].

The inherent challenges in detecting explosives arise from their diverse forms, ranging from conventional nitrate-based compounds to improvised explosive devices (IEDs) made from household materials. Generally, most of the explosives known today are nitroaromatics (NACs), with a few exceptions. Nitrobenzene (NB), Dintrobenzene (DNB), Trinitrobenzene (TNB), Nitrophenol (NP), Dinitrophenol (DNP), Trinitrophenol (TNP) or picric acid, Nitrotoluene (NT), Dinitrotoluene (DNT), Trinitrotoluene (TNT) and Nitroaniline (NA) are some of the well-known NACs which are major contributors to various explosive compositions [5] whereas dinitrodimethylbutane (DNMB), pentaerythritol tetranitrate (PETN), hexanitrohexaazaisowurtzitane (CL-20) and 1,3,5-trinitro- 1,3,5-triazinane (RDX) are not aromatic in nature [4]. These materials can be easily concealed in luggage, clothing, vehicles, or even on a person's body, making traditional security measures less effective. Moreover, the constantly evolving tactics employed by those seeking to exploit explosives for destructive purposes necessitate a continuous evolution of detection techniques [1]. As society becomes increasingly interconnected and dependent on critical infrastructure, the potential consequences of explosive-related incidents highlight the urgency of enhancing our ability to prevent and respond to such threats effectively [6].

In response to these challenges, scientists, engineers, and researchers have been working tirelessly to develop innovative methods for explosive detection. The need for accurate and reliable explosive detection goes beyond immediate security concerns. Previously, sensing of these high energy materials (HEMs) was carried out using instrumental techniques like ion mobility spectroscopy (IMS) [7], atmospheric pressure chemical ionization [8], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [9], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], plasma desorption mass spectrometry [12], Energy dispersive X-ray diffraction [13] and electrochemical methods [14,15]. These techniques offer high precision and compound-specific detection but are limited by the need for bulky equipment, high operational costs, skilled personnel, and susceptibility to interference in complex matrices. The inherent challenges posed by the low vapour pressures of most explosives, their chemical heterogeneity, and the emergence of unconventional explosive formulations further complicate detection. These limitations have fueled the demand for portable, cost-effective, and highly selective sensing platforms capable of operating in real-world environments. Among emerging technologies, fluorescence-based sensors have garnered particular interest due to their high sensitivity, rapid response, and compatibility with miniaturized, on-site detection devices [4,16].

Fluorescence-based detection has emerged as a powerful analytical approach for the identification of explosive materials. This method offers several critical advantages, including high sensitivity, rapid response times, and specificity in detecting a broad range of explosives. By utilizing fluorescent probes that undergo quenching or enhancement in the presence of target analytes, fluorometric sensing enables swift and accurate detection, even at trace levels. Recent advances in material design and photophysical tuning have further solidified this technique as a leading alternative to conventional detection technologies [16,17]. Recent reviews in the field of fluorometric detection of explosives have emphasized the significance of porous materials, particularly metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) [18–21]. In previous reports, the sensing capabilities of luminescent MOFs towards explosives have been highlighted, and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) active polymers have been explored for their detection performance [22,23]. Nanocomposites, molecular sensors [2,24–27], metal nanoclusters, and quantum dots [28,29] have been investigated in other reviews. The optoelectronic properties of coordination polymers (COPs) containing various metals and their sensing abilities towards explosives and other substances were studied using computational methods [30]. Fluorescence mechanisms and sensory materials, including conjugated polymers, supramolecules, small fluorophores, and bio-inspired materials, were also discussed previously [31,32]. A comprehensive summary of these previous efforts is provided in Table 1 , which collates a wide range of review articles focusing on the chemistry and application of distinct material categories in the detection of HEMs. These reviews collectively demonstrate the diverse and evolving nature of fluorometric sensing strategies.

While several reviews have addressed individual classes of fluorometric sensors for explosive detection, such as those based solely on small organic molecules, nanomaterials, or MOFs there remains a lack of a unified review that compares and contrasts performance, advantages, and limitations across multiple advanced functional material classes. This review seeks to bridge that gap by providing a broad yet curated overview of high-performance fluorometric sensors for explosive detection, covering molecular sensors, nanomaterials, polymer composites, coordination polymers (COPs), MOFs, and COFs. Rather than treating these materials as isolated entities, this review emphasizes the underlying photophysical mechanisms, design principles, and structure-function relationships that unify or differentiate their sensing behaviors (Fig. 1 ).

The novelty of this work lies in integrating insights from chemistry, materials science, nanotechnology, and biotechnology to explore the interplay of structure, emission properties, and molecular recognition in explosive detection. By doing so, it not only highlights recent advancements but also reveals opportunities for hybrid material development and synergistic design strategies. Additionally, this review addresses the current limitations and practical challenges faced by different material classes and outlines future directions to inspire the development of more effective, robust, and versatile sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of fluorescence in functional materials
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence in materials stems from several intrinsic photophysical processes that govern the nature and origin of their emissive behavior. One of the most common mechanisms is ligand-centered emission, where the fluorescence originates from π → π∗ or n → π∗ transitions within the organic ligand framework [33]. This type of emission is prevalent in conjugated systems with extended π-electron delocalization, such as aromatic or heteroaromatic rings, which facilitate efficient absorption of light and subsequent radiative relaxation from the excited singlet state to the ground state. Another key mechanism is metal-centered emission, which involves electronic transitions localized on the metal ion, such as spin-allowed or spin-forbidden d–d transitions in transition metal complexes or f–f transitions in lanthanide-based systems. Although d–d transitions are often weak due to Laporte-forbidden character, they can be partially allowed through vibronic coupling or symmetry distortion [34]. In contrast, f–f transitions in lanthanides, though parity forbidden, exhibit sharp emission bands and long lifetimes due to shielding by outer electrons, making them highly valuable for luminescence-based applications [4,35].

Additionally, charge-transfer mechanisms play a significant role in tuning the fluorescence properties of materials. These include metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT), where an electron is promoted from a metal-based orbital to an unoccupied ligand orbital upon excitation. Conversely, ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) involves electron donation from the ligand to an empty orbital on the metal center, often seen in high-valent transition metal complexes. Another variant is intra-ligand charge transfer (ILCT), wherein charge redistribution occurs between electron-rich and electron-deficient segments within the same ligand, often observed in push–pull systems incorporating donor–acceptor motifs. These charge-transfer transitions often result in broad, intense absorption bands and are highly sensitive to environmental factors such as solvent polarity, coordination geometry, and electronic configuration [33,36,37].

Aggregation-Induced Emission (AIE) is a photophysical phenomenon where certain organic luminophores exhibit enhanced fluorescence upon aggregation, contrary to the typical aggregation-caused quenching observed in many fluorescent materials. This behavior is primarily attributed to the restriction of intramolecular motions in the aggregated state, which suppresses nonradiative decay pathways and promotes radiative emission [38].

Excited-State Intramolecular Proton Transfer (ESIPT) is another photophysical process in which a proton within a molecule is transferred from a donor (commonly hydroxyl, amino, or carboxyl group) to an acceptor atom (typically a nitrogen or oxygen) in the excited state, resulting in a distinct tautomeric form. This process occurs extremely rapidly (within picoseconds) following photoexcitation. The ESIPT phenomenon leads to large Stokes shifts and dual emission behavior, where emission from both the normal and tautomeric forms can be observed [39]. In the detection of NACs, ESIPT-active luminophores have shown promise owing to their sensitive fluorescence response. The interaction of NACs with the proton donor or acceptor sites of ESIPT systems can disrupt or modulate the proton transfer process, resulting in a measurable shift or quenching of emission [40].

Fluorometric detection of NACs is a sensitive analytical approach that relies on fluorescence quenching to detect explosive compounds. HEMs often act as efficient quenchers by interacting with fluorophores, resulting in diminished emission intensity. The quenching process arises from energy or electron transfer mechanisms, governed by the electronic and structural compatibility between the fluorophore and the explosive [41,42]. Fluorescence quenching mechanisms can be broadly classified into (i) static and dynamic mechanisms, based on kinetic behavior, and (ii) electron and energy transfer pathways, based on the photophysical nature of the interaction.

Static quenching arises from the formation of a stable, non-emissive ground-state complex between the fluorophore and the quencher prior to excitation. This complex prevents the fluorophore from emitting upon excitation, resulting in reduced fluorescence intensity. A key feature of static quenching is that it does not alter the fluorescence lifetime, as only uncomplexed fluorophores contribute to emission. Evidence of static quenching can be supported by altered absorption spectra, Job's plot analyses, and a non-linear Stern Volmer Plot [43]. Dynamic quenching occurs due to collisional encounters between the excited-state fluorophore and the quencher, leading to non-radiative deactivation. This mechanism results in both decreased fluorescence intensity and reduced lifetime, since all fluorophores are susceptible to collisional deactivation during their excited-state lifetime. A linear Stern-Volmer plot indicates dynamic quenching [44].

PET involves the transfer of an electron from a donor to an acceptor molecule upon excitation. In the context of fluorescence sensing, the fluorophore (donor) transfers an electron to the analyte (acceptor), leading to quenching of fluorescence. This mechanism is particularly effective for detecting electron-deficient NACs, which act as strong electron acceptors. The efficiency of PET depends on the redox potentials of the donor and acceptor, as well as their spatial proximity [45,46].

Dexter electron exchange is a short-range mechanism that requires direct orbital overlap between the donor and acceptor molecules. It involves the simultaneous transfer of an electron from the donor to the acceptor and vice versa. This mechanism is highly distance-dependent, typically effective over distances less than 1 nm, and can lead to quenching of fluorescence when the acceptor is a non-fluorescent species [48,49].

FRET is a long-range, non-radiative dipole–dipole interaction between a donor fluorophore and an acceptor molecule. It is efficient when the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor and the two species are within 1–10 nm [50].

ICT occurs in molecules containing electron donor and acceptor moieties. Upon excitation, electron density is internally shifted, resulting in new emissive states. The presence of electron-withdrawing NACs can perturb the ICT balance, causing measurable shifts in emission intensity and wavelength. Bathochromic or hypsochromic shifts in emission spectra, often serve as an evidence for the same [51]. A schematic overview of the major energy and electron transfer mechanisms is presented in Fig. 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical and theoretical tools for mechanism elucidation
ce:section.ce:para:
A combination of analytical and theoretical techniques is employed to elucidate the underlying quenching mechanisms in fluorometric sensing. Stern–Volmer analysis is a fundamental tool used to evaluate quenching constants (Ksv), where linear plots typically indicate dynamic quenching, while deviations suggest static or mixed quenching behavior [52,53]. The Stern–Volmer plot, derived by fitting experimental data to the Stern–Volmer equation (Equation (1)), is also utilized to calculate the limit of detection (LOD) values using Equation (2).
(1)
I 0
I

K

(2)
