where I0 and I are the initial and final fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of the analyte, Ksv is Stern Volmer constant, [Q] is the concentration of the analyte, σ denotes the standard deviation of the blank, and m is the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot.

Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy (TRFS) is instrumental in measuring excited-state lifetimes, enabling clear distinction between static quenching, where lifetime remains unchanged and dynamic quenching, which results in a reduced lifetime [54]. Steady-state fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy aid in identifying spectral shifts associated with ICT or ground-state complex formation [55]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) offers insight into the redox potentials of both fluorophores and quenchers, which is essential for evaluating the feasibility of PET processes [56]. Computational approaches, such as density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT), are employed to predict electronic structures, including HOMO–LUMO energy levels, binding energies, and probable charge-transfer transitions [57]. Additionally, techniques like isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and Job's plot analysis are employed to determine the thermodynamics and stoichiometry of fluorophore-quencher interactions, particularly for static quenching pathways [58]. These complementary methodologies provide a comprehensive understanding of fluorescence quenching mechanisms in the detection of nitroaromatic explosives.

Functional materials are engineered with specific structural and physicochemical properties to meet the demands of targeted applications, such as explosive detection. In the context of chemosensors, these materials often integrate organic components offering tunable photophysical properties, and flexibility with inorganic constituents that contribute to stability, structural rigidity, or electronic conductivity. This synergistic combination enhances the sensitivity, selectivity, and robustness of the sensor systems, enabling effective detection of trace levels of HEMs [59,60].

Small organic molecules are low molecular weight compounds typically composed of aromatic or conjugated systems capable of exhibiting strong luminescence. Molecular chromophores are entities within molecules that absorb light in the UV–visible range and play a crucial role in optical sensing. In explosive sensing, their photophysical response, such as changes in emission intensity or wavelength can be directly correlated with the presence and concentration of target explosives. These changes arise from specific interactions, such as electron transfer or energy transfer, between the fluorophore and the analyte, enabling sensitive and quantifiable detection [61]. Mainly, conjugated π-systems, such as those found in conjugated double bonds or aromatic rings, and organic dyes are the widely employed molecules for sensing [62].

Tetraphenylethylene (TPE) derivatives exhibiting AIE have emerged as efficient fluorophores for NAC detection. THTPE, synthesized via McMurry and Suzuki coupling reactions, showed maximum AIE in aqueous media and enabled sensitive NAC detection (1.0 nM) via fluorescence quenching, with theoretical validation through DFT studies [63]. Similarly, a fluorescein-based fluorophore 2-((anthracen-9-ylmethylene) amino)-3′,6′-dihydroxyspiro [isoindoline-1,9′-xanthen]-3-one (ADX) demonstrated AIE behavior, notable Stokes shift, and dual-mode sensing for 4-nitroaniline (Turn-Off) and salicylaldehyde (Turn-On) in real water samples, supported by spectroscopic and computational analysis [64]. Triazine-based probes ITA and DIT, developed via Suzuki and Sonogashira couplings, displayed excellent solvatochromism, solid-state emission, and highly selective 4-NA sensing [65]. These fluorophores were effectively incorporated into paper strip sensors for portable, on-site detection. Across these studies, DFT calculations and charge transfer analyses corroborated the experimental sensing mechanisms. Another fluorescein-based sensors developed by Ilyas et al. revealed ultra low LODs of 0.73 and 1.7 nM for TNP detection [66].

Fluorene-based AIE active probes were synthesized through a single-step Knoevenagel condensation reaction, exhibiting excellent solvatochromism, reversible mechanochromism, and fluorescence quenching for the detection of 2,4-TNP in both solution and vapour phases. These probes demonstrated high sensitivity with low detection limits and were applied in real water sample analysis [67–69]. Additionally, triphenylamine-based probes with AIE enhancement properties showed sensitive fluorescence quenching for NB detection, further extending their application in environmental monitoring [70]. A stilbene-based sensor with red-shifted fluorescence emission was also developed for the selective and sensitive detection of nitrobenzene in solution and vapour phases. These sensors were successfully used for the fabrication of logic devices and on-site detection in real samples, showcasing their potential in practical applications [71].

Kumar et al. synthesized a series of five fluoranthene based derivatives (F1–F5) via Diels-Alder reaction followed by decarbonylation [72]. The synthetic scheme for the same was shown in Fig. 3 a. Owing to the presence of conjugation in fluoranthene unit and weak π-π interactions between two units, the crystals were seen to assemble into supramolecular arrangements. F5, 7,10-Di([1,1′-biphenyl]-4-yl)-8,9-bis(4- (hexyloxy)phenyl)fluoranthene, had a band gap of 2.88 eV and demonstrated extraordinary quenching ability as compared to other derivatives. The fluorescence quenching observed for NACs was attributed to electron transfer from LUMO of F5 to LUMO of the analyte. The molecule exhibited an LOD of 2–20 ppb for TNP, whereas 1.1 ppm for TNT and DNT. In another work, 7,10-bis(4-bromophenyl)-8,9-bis- [4-(hexyloxy)phenyl]fluoranthene was synthesized via a similar approach with a monoclinic P21/c space group (Fig. 3b), and displayed a LOD of 1.15 fg cm−2 [73].

Barot et al. developed a diphenothiazine based fluorescent schiff base for detection of TNT, DNP, DNB, 4-NP, and TNP. The sensor being hydrophobic in nature, demonstrated AIE with an increase in the amount of water. The probe exhibited the highest detection ability and selectivity for TNP due to hydrogen bonding between - CN groups and oxygen from picrate ions, as inferred from DFT studies [74]. Sandhu and co-workers fabricated 1-(p-terphenyl)-benzimidazolium based probe, TRIPOD-TP, which displayed aggregation induced fluorescence quenching on addition of aliquots of TNP, with a LOD of 0.5 pM [75]. The SEM images of the molecule before and after addition of TNP are showcased in Fig. 4 a-b. It was clearly observed that the rod like morphology of TRIPOD-TP was transformed to spherical aggregates with the addition of TNP. In another work, a conjugated curcumin based fluorophore (C1) was synthesized using N,N-Dimethyl benzaldehyde, tri-N-butylborate and N-butyl amine. C2 was developed via complexing boron difluoride with C1 [76]. C1 and C2 demonstrated selective detection ability towards TNP with LOD of 4.21 nM and 11.61 nM, respectively (Fig. 4c-d). The selectivity obtained was attributed to the enhancement of basicity of the molecules owing to the presence of –CH3 groups on N atom. This led to facile abstraction of a proton from TNP, resulting in N–H⋯O hydrogen bonding (Fig. 4e).

Zhang et al. employed 4-(diphenylamino)-benzaldehyde and 1, 3-diphenyl barbituronic acid to obtain 5-(4- Diphenylamino Styrene)-1,3-diphenyl barbituric acid with 90 % yield [77]. The molecule demonstrated AIE due to restriction of intramolecular movement in aggregates. This is attributed to limiting radiation decay leading to enhanced fluorescence emission, and is restricted to certain conformations like the Herringbone stacking model [78]. The formation of aggregates is ascribed to several non covalent interactions like C–H⋯O, C–O⋯N and π-π bonding, these interactions restrict intramolecular motion and hamper the non-radiative process. The molecule exhibited sensitive detection ability towards TNP, TNT, and DNT (Fig. 5 a) with a LOD of 2.4 μM towards TNP [77].

In several scientific reports, heterocyclic scaffolds have been employed for the detection of HEMs [2]. A pyridine based heterocyclic moiety, 3-Pyren-1-yl-1-pyridin-2-ylimidazo [1,5-a]pyridine was developed from 2,2′-dipyridyl ketone and pyrene-1-carbaldehyde and gave a LOD of 63 nM for TNP detection [79]. Other pyridine [80–82] and pyrimidine [83–86] based sensors have also been synthesized and reported earlier. Jiang et al. utilized a yellow solid, 3,3′-Didodecyl-2,2′-di(quinolin-2-yl)-3H,3′H-5,5′-bibenzo[d]imidazole to develop a sensor for selective recognition of TNP with LOD 4.86 ppb [87]. The Stern-Volmer plot of the same was as shown in Fig. 5b. The selective detection was explained due to the formation of aggregates between TNP and the molecule, following PET mechanism [88]. Tripheylene derivatives have also been reported as sensors for HEMs [89]. Verbitskiy and co-workers reported D-π-A type triphenylene amine based fluorophores, for detection of NACs in solution and vapour phase [90]. The synthesis scheme for two major derivatives, 2-[5-(4-Diphenylaminophenyl)thiophen-2-yl]pyrazine (7a) and 2-[5-(4-Diphenylaminophenyl) thiophen-2-yl]quinoxaline (7b) is depicted in Fig. 5c. The two fluorophores exhibited fluorescence quenching in presence of NACs.

The quenching mechanism was elucidated from the frontier molecular orbital theory and was found to be PET, from LUMO of fluorophore to LUMO of NAC. 2-[5-(4-Diphenylaminophenyl)thiophen-2-yl]pyrazine revealed remarkable response with highest quenching efficiencies across NACs (Fig. 5d) as compared to 2-[5-(4-Diphenylaminophenyl)thiophen-2-yl]quinoxaline (Fig. 5e). The LOD obtained for NB, TNP and TNT were 1.64 μM, 2.26 μM and 3.04 μM respectively, unlike 2-[5-(4-Diphenylaminophenyl)thiophen-2-yl]quinoxaline that had higher detection limits of 233 μM, 88.3 μM and 152 μM respectively. In a recent work, sodium (nitrilotris(benzene-4,1-diyl))tris(phosphonic acid), another triphenylene derivative demonstrated a low detection limit of 0.0535 μM of TNP, ascribed to hydrogen bonding between the sensor and analyte [91]. Duraimurugan et al. reported D-π-D, V-shaped triphenylamine based sensors, that demonstrated AIE [92]. They were successful in detection of TNP as a result of formation of a non-fluorescent charge transfer complex between the sensor and analyte [93]. In general, the greater sensitivity observed towards detection of TNP compared to other NACs has been ascribed to the loss of proton from the phenolic –OH trailed by anionic exchange with the sensor [94,95]. Other significant molecular sensors have been reviewed in the literature previously [62,96]. Table 2 summarizes important developments in molecular sensors for detection of NACs.

Hence, molecular sensors, particularly those based on conjugated organic fluorophores, offer high sensitivity and selectivity towards NACs. Their detection mechanisms often involve PET or FRET, leading to measurable fluorescence quenching upon analyte interaction. Advantages include facile synthesis, tunable photophysical properties, and the potential for real-time detection. However, challenges such as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and limited water solubility can impede performance. Recent developments in AIE fluorophores have addressed some of these issues, yet the need for enhanced selectivity and stability in complex environments remains.

Nanomaterials play a crucial role in explosive detection due to their unique properties, high surface area, and sensitivity to specific molecules. They can be functionalized with specific chemical groups or receptors that selectively interact with the molecules present in HEMs. This functionalization enhances the selectivity of the sensor by facilitating targeted interactions with the analytes of interest. Nanocomposites, consisting of nanomaterials combined with other materials, can be engineered to exhibit enhanced selectivity. By incorporating specific components that interact with the target HEMs, these nanocomposites can offer improved sensitivity and discrimination against interfering substances [97,98]. Nanomaterials with photoluminescent properties, such as quantum dots, can be designed to selectively quench or enhance their fluorescence in the presence of HEMs. This fluorescence modulation serves as a basis for selective detection [99]. Table 3 provides an overview of noteworthy developments in nanomaterials for detection of HEMs.

Carbon nanostructures, significantly carbon dots (CDs), have been explored for their potential in explosive detection. Changes in fluorescence intensity or wavelength in the presence of HEMs can be monitored for sensing purposes. Surface functionalization of CDs allows for selective binding with explosive molecules and interaction with specific chemical components of explosives, leading to detectable changes in properties such as electrical conductivity [100]. Carbon nanomaterial-based sensors can be miniaturized, making them suitable for portable and field-deployable explosive detection devices. Their small size and low power requirements contribute to the development of handheld or wearable detection systems [101].

Gunture et al. synthesized onion like nanocarbons obtained from diesel soot waste and functionalized it with oleylamine [102]. The synthesized nanocarbons showed detection ability towards DNP, 4-NP, and TNP. In line with other works, TNP was detected more effectively with a lower detection limit (1.4 μM), resulting in superior detection performance. Ferrari and co-workers utilized heptane as a carbon source to obtain multiwalled-carbon nanotubes via an aerosol assisted chemical vapour deposition technique [103]. The nanotubes were functionalized with amino groups in order to boost their interaction with electron deficient analytes, NACs. Garg et al. also synthesized hydrophobic carbon nanoparticles by reflux of maleic acid in presence of oleylamine. The material demonstrated high selectivity for TNP and DNP due to combined effect of FRET and PET mechanisms [104]. A rGO-based nanoprobe for selective detection of 4-NP and TNP in the liquid and gaseous phases. Fluorescence quenching was observed due to the inner filter effect (IFE) [105]. The activity of rGO hybrid materials for the same application has also been explored. As compared to rGO, hybrid materials with imine groups exhibited a greater quenching effect with NACs [106].

Lee et al. employed a hydrothermal approach to obtain CDs using uric acid and citric acid as precursors (Fig. 6 a) [107]. The detection of 2-NA, 4-NP, 3-NP, and DNP was as a result of FRET and IFE. FRET was justified on the basis of overlap observed between emission and absorption spectra of sensor and analyte, respectively. However, IFE was chosen on account of coinciding absorption spectra of analyte with the excitation or emission spectra of the probe [44,108]. In another work, CDs were synthesized by employing citric acid as a precursor and polyethylenimine (PEI) as a capping agent [109]. The detection of TNT proceeded via formation of a Meisenheimer type complex due to donation of electrons from amino groups in PEI to TNT [110].

Amphiphilic CDs with an average size of 23.3 nm have also been obtained using p-phenylenediamine and l-Histidine [111]. The CDs were used for real-time water sample analysis to detect the presence of TNP selectively (Fig. 6b). The LOD ranged from 0.23 to 1.31 μM, in different water samples and solvents. The quenching efficiency of TNP reached 95 % in acetone, which was the highest among the other used water samples and solvents. Niu and co-workers achieved a detection limit of 1 μM for TNP detection using CDs [112]. N-doped CDs derived from zeolites exhibited a low LOD of 0.36 μM towards TNP [113]. In another work, microwave assisted pyrolysis of malic acid was carried out to obtain N doped CDs [114]. CDs underwent a decrease in fluorescence intensity with addition of TNP and hence aided in the detection process. The detection mechanism followed was FRET and PET, owing to the formation of acid-base pair, with a LOD of 7.56 ppb. N-doped graphene quantum dots obtained from pyrolysis of lemon juice have been reported to give a low detection limit of 420 nM towards TNP [115]. In another work, graphene quantum dots obtained from white printing paper have also been used for detecting NACs, with a LOD of 0.5 μM for 2,4-DNP [116].

Although several carbon-based nanomaterials, including graphene derivatives and CDs, have been explored for explosive detection due to their exceptional surface area, electrical conductivity, and photoluminescent properties, functionalization with specific recognition elements can enhance selectivity towards explosive analytes. While these materials offer rapid response times and high sensitivity, challenges such as fluorescence quenching in aqueous environments, biocompatibility, and potential cytotoxicity need to be addressed.

Metal nanoparticles can influence the fluorescence of nearby fluorophores. In the presence of NACs, changes in the local environment around the nanoparticles can result in either enhanced fluorescence or quenching [117]. This phenomenon is exploited for sensitive fluorescence-based detection. Metal oxide nanocomposites, can provide synergistic effects, combining the unique characteristics of each component for improved detection capabilities.

Chen et al. demonstrated the fluorescent property of perovskite quantum dots CsPbX3 (X = Br, I) prepared via modified hot injection method [118]. The quantum dots were employed in the detection of TNP in organic phase. Studies were also carried out to study the interference due to the presence of other explosive compounds, and it was seen that the quenching efficiency was only slightly affected. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching was confirmed to be electrostatic electron transfer. Fig. 7 shows the results of quenching the fluorescent behaviour of CsPbBr3 and CsPbI3 quantum dots with TNP. The remarkable optical property of CsPbBr3 was exploited by creating a paper sensor by inkjet printing of the quantum dots on a membrane strip, which could be a boon to public safety and health in the near future [118].

In another work, an array of metal-based quantum dots were reported as sensors for NACs [119]. Au nanoclusters, MoS2 quantum dots, and WS2 quantum dots were synthesized by using a solvothermal technique. Au(0)@Au(I)thiolate (Au nanoclusters) are fluorescent in nature due to quantum confinement effect [120], AIE [120,121], LMCT [122], and minimal nonradiative transitions [123]. However, the clear reason behind fluorescent nature of MoS2 and WS2 has not been reported yet [124]. While conducting quenching studies of explosive materials, it was understood that Au nanoclusters undergo dynamic quenching, whereas the other two undergo static quenching. Photoluminescent N-doped CDs derived from guanine have also been used to detect 2,4-TNP [125]. Dual emitting Au nanoclusters (green and red) were also reported for the quantitative fluorometric detection of CL-20 and TNT [126]. A fluorescent paper sensor was reported for the detection of TNP and DNB with LOD 2.8 × 10- 7 M and 1.6 × 10- 7 M [127].

Dai et al. synthesized amorphous glutathione-functionalized copper nanoparticles (GSH-CuNPs by modulating solvent polarity and inhibiting crystallization through electrostatic repulsion. These phosphorescent nanostructures enabled sensitive TNT detection via PET through hydrogen bonding. The system exhibited high quantum yield (13.22 %), excellent selectivity, rapid response, and an ultralow detection limit of 1.56 pg for airborne TNT particulates [128]. In a work reported by D. Ayodhya, an Ag–CuO nanocomposite was capable of detecting TNP, DNP and 4-NP. 2,4-DNP effectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of composite with Ksv of 3.01 x 103 M−1 and LOD of 0.714 μM [129]. Bai and co-workers synthesized ZnS:Mn2+ hydrophobic quantum dots and then prepared ZnS:Mn2+ @allyl mercaptan composite via in-situ polymerization. The composite was employed for sensitive detection of TNT and TNP with LOD 0.01 and 0.05 μg mL−1, respectively (Fig. 8 a) [130].

Chen and co-workers synthesized Tb doped CDs, via carbonization technique by utilizing Tb(NO3)3.5H2O and citric acid as precursors (Fig. 8b) [131]. The nanocomposite revealed selective TNP detection ability following a combination of PET, FRET and inner filter effect mechanisms. Another fluorescent probe, PEI capped KMgF3:Eu2+ nanoparticles, behaved as donors with NACs as acceptor moieties [132]. The nanocomposite, together with NACs formed a Meisenheimer complex thereby leading to quenching of fluorescence. The process followed a FRET mechanism owing to the overlap between donor absorption spectra and acceptor emission spectra. Fig. 8c depicts the simulated charge transfer complex formed between KMgF3:Eu2+ and TNT, with a distance of ∼10 nm between them. The short distance between the two also accounted for FRET mechanism of quenching. KMgF3:Eu2+ nanoparticles demonstrated a LOD of 0.481, 0.276, 0.483, 0.409 and 0.485 ng mL−1 for TNT, 4-NT, TNP, DNB and NB, respectively in DI water [132]. Other nanocomposites like CdSe/ZnS quantum dots [133], CdTe/ZnS quantum dots [134], amine-capped ZnS–Mn2+ [135], YF3:Yb3+/Er3+ [136], ZnO [137] have been reviewed previously.

Metal nanoclusters particularly those composed of noble metals like gold and silver, exhibit size-dependent fluorescence and have been utilized for detecting explosives such as TNP. They are known for their ultra-small size, high photostability, and the ability to undergo surface modifications for enhanced selectivity. However, the synthesis of these nanoparticles with consistent size and emission properties can be challenging, and their fluorescence may be susceptible to environmental factors. Nanocomposites, formed by combining these nanoparticles with polymers or other matrices, leverage synergistic effects to improve explosive detection capabilities. These composites can offer improved sensitivity and selectivity; however, issues related to reproducibility, scalability, and long-term stability need to be systematically investigated.

Polymer-based materials and their composites play a crucial role in the development of sensors for detecting explosives due to their tuneable properties, cost-effectiveness, high quantum yield, synthetic versatility and ease of fabrication. These composites typically consist of a polymer matrix reinforced with filler materials that enhance the sensor's sensitivity and selectivity [138]. Owing to their flat surface, polymers are generally not penetrated with the analytes, NACs. On conversion of these conjugated chains/sheets to nanoparticles, their properties are further enhanced due to facile functionalization and increased solubility [139].

Cao et al. prepared polyacrylonitrile nanofibers functionalized with pyrene (Py-PAN NFM), via green approach [140]. The FESEM images of the synthesized material are as seen in Fig. 9 a and b. PAN NFM was obtained by electrospinning, and pyrene was introduced on the surface by using 1-aminopyrene in deep eutectic solvents. The surface of Py-PAN NFM, being hydrophobic, was subjected to extraction with dichloromethane in order to change sample polarity. The blue light emission shown by Py-PAN NFM, in the visible range, underwent a decrease in intensity with the addition of TNT and 2,4-DNT. The quenching constants were found to be 2.57 × 106 M− 1 and 3.15 × 105 M− 1 with LOD 8.81 × 10- 9 M and 2.74 × 10- 8 M, respectively. From the Stern-Volmer plots (Fig. 9c and d), it was clearly inferred that PET led to the quenching process [140]. In another work, PAN based fibres, incorporated with 3,6-Diaminocarbazole exhibited excellent results for vapour phase detections of DNT and TNT [141]. Ouyang and co-workers synthesized linear and cross-linked PAnTPE polymer nanoparticles by employing tetraphenylethene (TPE) and 9,10-Bis(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolan-2-yl)anthracene [142]. Owing to IFE, the polymer nanoparticles exhibited selectivity towards TNP detection with LODs 663 and 439 nM for the linear and crosslinked polymers, respectively. The low LOD obtained for crosslinked PAnTPE was ascribed to its porous structure, aiding in better adsorption and diffusion of the analyte.

Tawfiq and co-workers developed biocompatible nano sensors, namely cationic polythiophene nanohybrids (CPTQDs), anionic polythiophene nanohybrids (APTQDs), nonionic polythiophene nanohybrids (NPTQDs), and thiophene copolymer nanohybrids (TCPQDs) via an in situ polymerization method [143]. The use of polythiophene ligands resulted in multi-coloured sensors having different fluorescence emissions and good quantum yield. Fig. 9e shows the photoluminescence spectra of the different nanohybrids synthesized. The presence of hydrophobic π-conjugated structures as backbones and hydrophilic groups led to polythiophene structures, which were amphiphilic in nature. This imparted remarkable photophysical properties, water solubility, photostability, high sensitivity, low cytotoxicity and high quantum efficiency. The incorporation of Cd Te quantum dots enhanced thermal stability and prevented aggregation. This also led to a significant enhancement in the electron-rich nature of the synthesized material. Thus, electron-deficient TNP could interact effectively with electron-rich sensors, thereby resulting in selective detection (Fig. 9f). Paper sensors made with TCPQD could detect TNP with concentrations as low as 10−9 M. The paper sensor was also coupled with a smartphone for easy practical use [143].

Shan and team fabricated methyl ammonium lead tribromide (MAPbBr3) implanted in poly(vinylidene fluoride) matrix (PVDF) via freeze-drying (Fig. 10 a). The SEM images reveal an open-porous structure of the perovskite composite (Fig. 10b-c) [144]. The quenching shown by the composite on addition of NACs was attributed to an increase in trap-density, excluding the commonly observed PET mechanism [145].

Patel and co-workers labelled starch nanoparticles with 1-pyrenebutyric acid and used it for selective detection of HEMs NT, DNT and TNT with LOD 9.2, 3.3, and 0.2 ng mm−2, respectively [146]. From the Stern-Volmer plot, it was inferred that a protective quenching mechanism was followed, whereby a part of the pyrene labels was not accessible to the quencher. In another work, a polyaniline (PANI) based composite, PANI-Ag was synthesized via chemical oxidation method, by using ammonium persulphate as the oxidant [147]. The formation of the composite was confirmed from XRD pattern (Fig. 11 a), with the characteristic peaks of Ag at 2θ = 32.1°, 44°, 67°, and 76°. The origin of fluorescence in PANI-Ag was attributed to π-π∗ transitions owing to benzenoid rings in polymer chains. The composite exhibited detection ability towards TNP (Fig. 11b) and DNB with a lower LOD for the former (0.127 ppm) as compared to the latter (3.916 ppm). The overlap between absorption spectra of TNP and emission spectra of the composite indicated the possibility of FRET mechanism (Fig. 11c).

In another work, lanthanide complexes incorporated luminescent hybrid hydrogel (PVA–PC–Tb) were synthesized by Xie and co-workers [148]. The hydrogels were made from polyvinyl alcohol, modified chitosan, and TbCl3.Chitosan modified with pyridine-2,3-dicarboxylic acid showed strong coordination with Tb3+ via antenna effect, which manifested in greater photoluminescence with sharp emission peaks at 490, 544, 584, and 622 nm. The hydrogels also had excellent mechanical properties like stretchability, elasticity, and toughness [148]. Shaligram et al. synthesized polybenzimidazole 5-tert-butyl isophthalic acid polymer (PBI-BuI) and further modified it into polymeric ionic liquids thin films, [DPyDBzPBI-BuI][Br] and [DAnDBzPBI-BuI][Br] (Fig. 12 a) [149]. The thin films demonstrated quenching with NACs. The added advantage of using these thin films was the recovery of fluorescent intensity on washing the films with methanol. Porous organic polymer, tetraphenyl-5,5-dioctylcyclopentadiene (TPDC-DB) synthesized via Sonogashira cross coupling demonstrated a LOD of 455 ppb for 4-NA (Fig. 12b) [150]. A tri-channel fluorescence array using gold nanoclusters and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide-based probes was developed by Gao and co-workers for simultaneous detection of seven explosives [151]. The system was integrated into a portable polymer hydrogel-based sensor for visual and quantitative field detection. Distinct fluorescence color changes enabled rapid identification, while quantitative analysis was achieved via linear discriminant analysis and smartphone integration. This hydrogel-based device demonstrated strong potential for practical, portable explosive sensing applications.

Akkoc and Karagoz developed poly(4-(1-pyrenyl)-styrene-coethyleneglycoldimethacrylate), P(PySt-EGDMA) microspheres that showed strong fluorescence at concentrations as low as 6.67 μg mL−1 [152]. From the DLS analysis, the microbeads had a z-average size of 3200 nm with a polydispersity index of 0.469. The microbeads could be dispersed in both aqueous and organic media, thereby showing good detection ability. Surprisingly, DNT demonstrated a better quenching effect as compared to TNT. This was explained by the less steric hindrance in DNT [153], which promoted its ease of diffusability in the microspheres [127]. Kumar et al. reported alanine based dansyl tagged copolymer (DCP), which showed fluorescence emission between 400 and 650 nm [154]. It was seen that the emission spectra of DCP overlapped with absorption spectra of TNP. This resulted in the conclusion that a resonance energy transfer mechanism was followed, and it took place from DCP to TNP. The high acidity of TNP made it easier to detect [154]. In a work reported earlier, P(MMA-co-TCMA) was prepared via copolymerization of methyl methacrylate (MMA) and 7- (4-trifluoromethyl) coumarin-N-methacrylamide (TCMA) with quenching constant 0.259 × 104 M−1 and 1.399 × 104 M−1 for DNT and TNT, respectively [155]. Zheng et al. synthesized 3, 6-diethynyl carbazole based linear polymer (P1) and a hyperbranched conjugated polymer (P2), obtained via Sonogashira cross coupling between 1, 3, 6-atriyne carbazole(3,6-diethynyl carbazole) and 3,6-dibromo-9-hexyl carbazole [156]. Due to greater intramolecular steric hindrance in the latter, its photoluminescence spectra exhibited hypsochroic shift (Fig. 12c). Owing to multiple electron diffusion pathways, a higher quenching constant with NACs was obtained for the conjugated polymer as compared to the linear chain (Fig. 12d). Adamantane-pyrene based copolymer, also prepared via Sonogashira cross-coupling, exhibited a LOD of 10.44 μM for TNT [157].

A series of degradable, highly emissive polycarbonates incorporating AIE-active monomers were synthesized via epoxide/CO2 copolymerization, achieving tunable emission from blue to yellow [158]. Among them, a polymer, P-TCN, enabled a high-performance white light-emitting diode device with an external quantum efficiency of 26.09 %. Additionally, these polymers served as selective fluorescent sensors for nitroaromatic compounds in their nanoaggregate states. Other polymers based on calixarenes [159,160], tetraphenyl silane [161,162], polythiophene [163,164], polsiloxane [165] have also been employed for the detection of NACs. Glackin et al. presented a sensitive method for detecting trace explosive residues using swabs coated with a fluoropolymer to collect explosives, followed by thermal desorption and fluorescence quenching with Super Yellow sensors [166]. The technique, tested with 2,4-DNT, showed up to three orders of magnitude higher sensitivity compared to colorimetric tests. Field tests on military-grade explosives (TNT, PETN, RDX) demonstrated the potential of organic semiconductors for sub-milligram explosive detection from real-world samples.

Conjugated polymers have been extensively studied for explosive sensing due to their delocalized π-electron systems, which facilitate efficient charge transport and fluorescence. Their structural versatility allows for the design of materials with specific interactions towards explosive analytes. Advantages include solution processability and the potential for large-area sensor fabrication. Nevertheless, challenges such as photobleaching, environmental degradation, and limited selectivity in complex matrices persist. Developing polymers with enhanced stability and specificity remains a key research focus.

Porous materials are substances with pores or voids, which can be either open (connected) or closed (disconnected). The significance of these materials lies in their ability to control and manipulate the flow of fluids through their structure. They offer a versatile platform for a wide range of applications, enabling engineers and scientists to tailor their properties for specific needs. The ability to control the size, shape, and distribution of pores allows for customization and optimization in diverse fields, making them highly significant in various industries [167]. Table 4 provides a summary of significant porous materials reported for explosive detection.

MOFs are hybrid crystalline solids consisting of metal ions with organic linkers [19]. The choice of organic ligands in MOF plays a key role in deciding its luminescence properties. Fluorescent MOFs, also known as luminescent MOFs, exhibit unique optical properties, including fluorescence, phosphorescence, or even chemiluminescence, which can be harnessed for the development of explosives detection platforms [168]. The development of MOF-based fluorescent sensors for explosive detection involves careful design and synthesis of MOF structures, selection of appropriate fluorophores, and optimization of the sensing conditions. Researchers focus on tailoring the MOF properties to achieve high selectivity, sensitivity, and stability in detecting specific explosive molecules [37]. Luminescent MOFs have been exploited in chemo-sensing due to their ease of synthesis, tuneable and easy induced luminescence, quick response time, and simple operability [169,170].

Wu and co-workers reported a 2D-pincer MOF [L4Cd3(H2O)2]n (H2L = 1,3-(bis(6-(2-methyl)nicotinyl))benzene) [171] where each ligand was linked to [Cd(COO)2]n chains to give rise to a dual layered 2D sheet. During fluorescence quenching, the MOF showed a quenching constant of 6.6 x 104 M−1 with LOD of 0.119 μM for TNP. It was seen that other explosives like NP, DNT, NB showed almost negligible effects in fluorescence quenching, whereas TNP demonstrated complete quenching (Fig. 13 a) [171]. Another Cd-based MOF, {[Cd2(L4−)(H2O)6]·2H2O}n was synthesized using 3-(3′,5′-dicarboxyphenyl)pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid (H4L) and Cd(NO3)2·4H2O via a solvothermal method [172]. The MOF exhibited strong fluorescent emission at 472 nm, which was attributed to intra-ligand or ligand-to-ligand charge transfer transitions [173]. From the Stern-Volmer plot, it was inferred that the quenching ability of NACs varied in the order TNP >2,4-DNP > 4- NP > 2-NP > NB. However, the LOD of these explosives showed a reverse order [172]. SNNU-111, a Cd-based framework, containing 1,2,4,5-Benzene tetracarboxylic acid and 3-Mercaptotriazole showed photoluminescence both in solution as well as solid state, which was ascribed to intra-ligand charge transfer and π-π stacking [174]. The MOF was successful in detecting explosives like NB, 1,2-DNB, 2,4-DNT, 1,3-DNB, TNT, and TNP with highest Ksv value for NB (Fig. 13b) [174].

Hu et al. synthesized trifluoromethyl TMU-44, [Zn2(hfipbb)2(L1)]n.DMF, L1 = N,N′-bis-pyridin-4-ylmethylene-benzene-1,4-diamine), H2hfipbb = 4,4′-(hexafluoroisopropylidene) bis(benzoic acid), and TMU- 45, [Zn2(hfipbb)2(L2)]n, DMF, (L2 = N,N′-bis-pyridin-4-ylmethylene-naphthalene-1,5-diamine) and exploited them for their luminescence property [175]. The synthesized MOFs demonstrated selective detection towards phenolic NACs. The trifluoromethyl group underwent C–F⋯H–O hydrogen bonding with the phenolic groups and thus selectively detected phenolic NACs. This selective detection was completely attributed to the host-guest chemistry between the MOF and the explosive compound [175]. In another work, an ultrasonic method for synthesis of Zn based MOF nanorods was employed [176]. The FESEM image of the nanorods is seen in Fig. 13c. The incorporation of urea inside the MOF pore cavity led to remarkable detection of TNP at a faster rate as compared to other previously reported works [176]. In another work, 3D porous material (BMI)2[Zn3(ptptc)2] (BMI = 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium, H4ptptc = p-terphenyl-3,3″,5,5″- tetracarboxylic acid) consisting of a Zn MOF directed by [BMI]Br ionic liquid was reported for detection of NACs in aqueous medium [177]. The use of solvothermal and ionothermal techniques gave rise to trinuclear Zn(II) clusters. In order to confirm resonance energy transfer as the mechanism for fluorescence quenching, it is necessary to check an effective overlap between absorption band of analyte and the emission band of MOF [178,179]. 1,3-bis(1-imidazolyl)benzene (bib) and 4,4′-oxydibenzoic acid (4,4′-H2oba) were employed as ligands to synthesize Zn based MOFs. {[Zn(bib)(oba)]∙2H2O}n and {[Zn2(bib)2(oba)2]∙2EtOH∙H2O}n synthesized solvothermally, were selectively sensitive to electron deficient NACs and exhibited anti-interference abilities. They were seen to quench fluorescence following an electron transfer mechanism. A double layer cavity and a dual chain structure, respectively, were seen by the 2D MOFs [180]. {[Zn2(pydc)2(DMF)]⋅1.5DMF}n (H2pydc = pyridine-2,5-dicarboxylic acid) synthesized solvothermally gave fairly good results for detection of explosives with a LOD 0.12 ppb for TNP [181]. Zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) doped with 8-hydroxyquinoline zinc (ZnQ) showed visible light change from ivory to light red on exposure to TNT [182]. ZnQ@ZIF-8 was also coated and made into paper sensors, which exhibited fluorescence quenching at 455 nm at 1 ppm TNT concentration. In liquid phase, TNT concentration as low as 100 ppb was successfully detected. The incorporation of ZnQ guest into ZIF-8 gave rise to large crystal size distribution ranging from 300 nm to 900 nm. The SEM image of the prepared sample is given in Fig. 13d [182]. {(NH2(CH3)2)[Zn4(ddn)2(COO)(H2O)4].solvent}n, another Zn-based MOF with a 3D (3,4,6)-c connected net demonstrated a very low detection limit of 1.12 ppm towards DNP [183].

Xie et al. demonstrated nanosized square channels with a 3,6 connected network in [(Zn4O)(H2O)2(TPA)2]·8DMA (H3TPA = 4,4′,4″-nitrilotrisbenzoic acid; DMA = N, N-dimethylacetamide) [184]. The synthesized MOF gave remarkable results for sensing of NACs. Fig. 14 a–d shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the 3D MOF in presence of explosives [184]. Liu and team extensively studied the role of hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking in the MOF, Zn2(L)(bipy)(H2O)2].(H2O)3(DMF)2 in electron transfer during the detection process by investigating the crystal and cluster structure of the MOF [185]. The studies concluded a robust electronic coupling between NB and the MOF that resulted in photo-excitation of valence electrons in the MOF to LUMO of NB (Fig. 14e). FJI-H15, comprising 5,5′,5''-((1,3,5-triazine-2,4,6-triyl)tris(oxy))triisophthalic acid as ligand demonstrated reduction in fluorescence intensity in presence of phenolic NACs, following a combination of electrostatic interaction and energy transfer mechanisms [186].

Pal and co-workers reported the synthesis and structural topology of {[Cd2(L)(L1)(DMF)(H2O)](2DMF)(3H2O)}n and {[Cd4(L)2(L2)3(H2O)2](8DMF)(8H2O)}n where L = [1,1′:3′,1″-terphenyl]-4,4′,4″,6′-tetracarboxylic acid, L1 = 4,4′- bipyridine and L2 = 2-amino-4,4′-bipyridine acted as linker and co-linkers respectively [187]. The two Cd based MOFs were fabricated in a Teflon-lined autoclave with a mixture of DMF and ethanol as solvents for 72 h at 100 °C. The former complex comprised coordination of on Cd atom with four oxygen atoms (two molecules of L) and two nitrogen atoms (two molecules of L2) and the other Cd atom with four oxygen atoms (two molecules of L), one oxygen atom from H2O and one nitrogen atom from L2. The mentioned coordination manifested in the form of a single helical chain that wrapped around each other via weak π interactions to form a double helix. Each double helix underwent interconnection through L2, resulting in a three dimensional framework (Fig. 14f). The latter MOF had a quenching constant of 3.89 × 104 M−1 for TNP detection at room temperature, with a LOD of 1.98 ppm. Theoretical calculations were simulated for PET and FRET between {[Cd2(L)(DMF)3]·0.5DMF}n (H4L = terphenyl-3,3″,5,5″-tetracarboxylic acid) and NACs leading to quenching [188].

Moscoso et al. employed lanthanide based MOFs for detecting NACs in the vapour phase [189]. Tb(BTC)@PMMA (BTC-Benzene tricarboxylate, PMMA- Polymethyl-methacrylate) films produced by depositing the Ln-MOF on a glass substrate by using PMMA as matrix, had high structural stability and metallic open sites which have the potential for sensing applications [190]. The sensitivity of detection of explosives followed the order TNP > TNT > DNT > DNB > DNMB. High sensitivity for TNP was attributed to greater electron withdrawing nature and strong ability to garner electrons via photoinduced electron transfer mechanism [189]. In a work reported elsewhere, [Tb(L1)2/3(L2)1/2(H2O)2].2H2O (where H3L1 = 2,4,6-tris(4-carboxyphenoxy)-1,3,5-triazine, H2L2 = terephthalic acid) was also employed for sensing NACs [191]. Another lanthanide based MOF, In/Eu-CBDA and In/Tb-CBDA (CBDA = 5,5’-(carbonylbis(azanediyl))-diisophthalic acid) demonstrated detecting ability towards 1,4-DNB probably due to LMCT and resonance energy [192].

Sun and team explored [Eu2L2(DMF)(H2O)2].2DMF.H2O (H3L = 5-(1-carboxynaphthalen-4-yl)benzene-1,3-dioic acid) as a multifunctional sensor detecting 4-NP in a concentration as low as 3.63 ppm [193]. La(HTCPE)(H2O)2 (HTCPE = tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)ethylene), a 3D MOF gave remarkable sensitivity and selectivity for NA detection with Ksv value of 3.24 104 M−1 [194]. Ti6(μ3-O)6(μ2-OH)6(BTCA)2(DMF)2 (ZSTU-3) has also been reported with a comparatively higher Ksv value of 4.253 104 M−1 for TNP [195].

Yang and co-workers used 1,3,5-Benzene tribenzoic acid and ZrCl4 to prepare Zr-MOF, which was encapsulated with rhodamine B (RhB) to give RhB@Zr-MOFc [196]. The multifunctional, self-calibrating fluorescence sensor had dual emission properties and was capable of detecting not only explosives but also pesticides and cations. Fig. 15 shows the mechanism of fluorescence quenching in RhB@Zr-MOF [196].

A Zr based MOF (synthesis as shown in Fig. 16 a, CJLU-1 (Zr6(μ3-O)4(μ3- OH)4(OH)6(TCA)2(H2O)6) (H3TCA = tri-carboxylic acids 4,4′,4″-nitrilotribenzoic acid) demonstrated a detection limit of 83 ppb for TNP [197]. The 2D porous structure had a nanoflower like structure as inferred from the SEM (Fig. 16b) and TEM images (Fig. 16c). The explosives showed quenching, decreasing in the order 2,4-TNP > 4-NP > 4- NB > 2,4-DNT >2,6-DNT, which was on the same lines as that reported by Sun and co-workers [172]. Another Zr based MOF, NU-1000-NH2, behaved as a good fluorescent sensor due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding N–H—Oanalyte between the donor MOF and acceptor NACs. The synthesized MOF was quenched by explosives in the order TNT >2,4-DNT >3,4-DNT > 3-NT > 2,6-DNT > NB > 4-NT > 2-NT. From the theoretical calculations, it was revealed that photoinduced electron transfer mechanism was followed [198].

Wang et al. recently reported TNP as a quencher for a synthesized MOF, [Mg2(H2O)4TCPP].DMF·5CH3CN (Mg-TCPP, TCPP = tetra- (4-carboxyphenyl)pyrazine) with Ksv = 3.63 × 105 L/mol and a LOD as low as 25.6 ppb [199]. A Cu MOF was reported by Zhang and co-workers, for detection of Na salt of NP with a detection limit as low as 0.0651 μM [200]. In the work reported by Tang et al., UIO-66-NH2 was modified with tert-butyl nitrite and azido trimethyl silane in tetrahydrofuran to form UIO-66-N3. UIO-66-N3 was further modified with 1-ethynylpyrene to give UIO-66-Py [201]. It was seen that, UIO-66-Py demonstrated a significant fluorescent enhancement as compared to UIO-66-NH2, which was attributed to the introduction of pyrenyl units and conversion from azide to triazole units after modification. UIO-66-Py reported a quenching efficiency of 85 % with the addition of TNP in 150 s. It was concluded that, triazole units formed hydrogen bonds with TNP, thereby supporting a photo induced electron transfer mechanism [201]. Another –NH2 functionalized MOF, MIL-101 (Fe) afforded an excellent detection limit of 9.8 ppm for 4-NT [202]. Although several luminescent MOFs have demonstrated the ability to detect NACs with high sensitivity and selectivity, issues such as moisture sensitivity, complex synthesis and framework stability under operational conditions need to be addressed to realize practical applications.

COFs are porous, crystalline organic solids having repeating units of covalently bonded organic molecules [203]. The synthesis of COF involves simultaneous polymerization and crystallization, obeying the principle of dynamic covalent chemistry [204]. The wide range of applications offered by COF is attributed to the post-synthetic modification of functional groups present in the COF [205]. They find applications in bio-imaging, photocatalysis, gas adsorption, drug delivery, energy conversion, and storage [21,206]. COFs are also rarely employed for fluorescence-based sensing applications due to the accumulation of analytes between their layers (2D COF) or pores (3D COF) [207]. The key to successful explosive detection using COFs lies in achieving high sensitivity and specificity. COFs should be designed in a way such that their functional groups selectively bind to explosives over other potential interfering compounds, and the fluorescence response is strong enough to detect trace amounts of explosives. The organic linkers may sometimes undergo rotation, thereby quenching fluorescence. Thus, this limits the application of COF in sensing [207,208].

Wang and co-workers synthesized a fluorescent Pyrene-Azine COF combined with MnO2 microspheres and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The COF micromotors demonstrated autonomous motion in the solution. The synthesized COF comprised open docking sites for guest molecules. This was due to the presence of a columnar ordered structure of pyrene units and incorporation of azine units in pore walls. The COF showed high sensitivity towards fluorescent quenching by TNP. This was attributed to greater hydrogen bonds between NO2 groups in TNP and azine units. The microsensors could be used in aqueous media with high sensitivity in less time [209]. Gao et al. also reported a pyrene COF, Py-TPE-COF, with 1,1,2,2- tetrakis(4-formylphenyl)ethane (TPE aldehyde) as the AIE unit. The COF was spherical with a size ranging from 300 to 500 nm and demonstrated a detection limit of 1 ppm for TNP [210]. In another work, azine linked COF has been computationally evaluated for selective sensing of NACs [211].

Faheem et al. assembled 4,4′,4″,4‴-(ethene-1,1,2,2-tetrayl) tetraaniline (ETTA) and 9,10-anthracenedicarboxaldehyde to form a COF with high chemical as well as thermal stability [212]. The rhombic shaped sheets as seen from the SEM images (Fig. 17 a and b), were formed by tetraphenylene units at the vertices, and anthracene units at the edges connected via CN linkages. The presence of anthracene units and ETTA led to π-π interactions, thereby showing fluorescence via electron and energy transfer [213]. The presence of imine groups enhanced the probability of hydrogen bonding with guest molecules, thus boasting selective detection [214]. Fig. 17c shows the quenching efficiency of TNP for ETTA based COF. From the Stern-Volmer plots (Fig. 17d), it was inferred that the dual luminescent COF followed a combination of both static and dynamic quenching as well as energy and electron transfer with TNP. The LOD for TNP was found to be 13.10 ppb [212].

Jiang et al. synthesized Pythz-COF and Pyaph-COF with terephthalic dihydrazide (Thz) and 4-aminobenzohydrazide (APH) as linkers, respectively [215]. Pythz-COF exhibited better sensing ability than Pyaph-COF, indicating the significance of hydrazine structure in sensing. The presence of hydrazine units promotes hydrogen bonding interaction of COFs with nitro group in explosives, thus leading to their selective detection [216]. Fig. 17e and f shows Stern-Volmer plots of Pythz-COF and Pyaph-COF against concentration of TNP [215].TNP was also detected by another COF, PTCF-8, comprising a triazine framework. The COF demonstrated excellent thermal stability and was successful in sensing TNP with a LOD 1 μM [217]. Shan et al. reported a stair-type structure for PCP- COF comprising a Z-shaped unit, [2.2]-paracyclophane (PCP) [218]. The COF obtained via a bottom-up approach, was found to have a large BET surface area of 1123 m2g-1, accounting for its remarkable performance in fluorescence quenching. From the discussion, it is clear that the ordered structures and tunable functionalities in COFs enable the design of sensors with high specificity towards explosive molecules. COFs offer advantages such as thermal stability, low density, and the potential for post-synthetic modifications. However, their relatively low crystallinity and limited processability can hinder sensor fabrication and performance. Advancements in COF synthesis and processing techniques are essential to overcome these challenges.

Coordination polymers are hybrid structures consisting of inorganic metal cation centres linked to organic ligands via a coordination bond. They are formed when several 1-dimensional coordination entities form cross links, giving rise to 2-dimensional structures. They are often formed when ligands chosen, have the ability to form multiple coordinate bonds, thereby acting as a bridge [30,219,220]. The 2-dimensional structure of a coordination polymer may often undergo structural transformation due to hydrogen bonding, π-stacking, dipole-dipole interaction, and van der Waals interaction, thereby giving a 3-dimensional structure [221]. Interaction between the explosive molecules and the COPs can result in fluorescence quenching or enhancement.

Wang et al. employed lanthanide COP spheres as multi-modal sensors for the detection of NACs. A hydrothermal method employing Ru[4,4’–(COOH)2–bpy]3 & Cl2 (L) and GdCl3 & 6H2O was used to synthesize the COP spheres. The strong luminescent property observed in COP spheres was understood by guest-host transfer mechanism. The fluorescent COP spheres reported here showed a significantly low LOD of 0.13 ppm and 0.31 ppm for TNT and TNB, respectively, as compared to other previously reported works [222]. In another work, [Sr3(BPTC)1.5(H2O)6.5]n (H4BPTC = biphenyl-3,3′,5,5′-tetracarboxylic acid) synthesized via hydrothermal route behaved as a bifunctional sensor for both Fe3+ and 4-NP showing a quick response and high sensitivity [223]. A Ba based COP, [Ba(Nyaia)(H2O)]n (H2Nyaia = 5-[(naphthalene-1-methylene)amino] isophthalic acid) synthesized solvothermally was also reported for the same analytes with a Ksv of 4.44 × 105 M− 1 for 4-NP [224].

Eu (III) and Tb (III) with bis-terpyridine as ligand were employed for the synthesis of COPs via sonication [225]. The synthesized nanosheets as confirmed from FE SEM images, were ultrathin with a thickness of 2–6.5 nm. The combination of both lanthanides in different stoichiometries resulted in different coloured emissions. The 2D nanosheets showed excellent activity in detection of insensitive high energy explosive, 1,1-diamino-2,2- dinitroethene (FOX-7). Fig. 18 a shows a schematic illustration of the synthesis and detective property of Eu(III) and Tb(III) based COP [225]. In another work, [Tb(HL)3 (CH3OH)(H2O)]n.H2O and [Eu(HL)3 (CH3OH)2 ]n, where H2L = 3- (picolinamido)benzoic acid) were fabricated and employed for size dependent sensing of HEMs [226]. The three-dimensional architecture composed of hydrophobic channels aided in the interaction of Lewis-basic sites with Lewis-acidic NACs.

Zhao et al. fabricated {[Cd2(obtz)(Meip)2].H2O}n and {[Cd(obtz)(ndc)].0.5H2O}n (obtz = 1,2-bis(1,2,4-triazole-1-ylmethyl)benzene, Meip = 5-methylisophthalate, ndc = 1,4-naphthalenedicarboxylate) having a 4,5 connected 3D and 3,4 connected 2D network respectively [227]. The quenching efficiency shown by the polymers at different concentrations of explosive materials is shown in Fig. 18b-c. The absorption spectra of 4-NP and 4-NA showed effective overlap with the emission spectra of the COPs whereas NB and 1,3-DNB showed almost negligible overlap resulting in the as observed quenching efficiencies. In another work, [Cd(glu)2(pbiq)2(H2O)]n, [H2glu = glutaric acid and pbiq = 4-(6-(pyridin-4-yl)benzo [4,5]imidazo [1,2-c]quinazoline)] prepared via in-situ oxidation was reported to give a LOD of 1.51 × 10−7 M for TNP [228].

In another work, a 2D layered COP, Cd-TTPBA-4, [Cd(TTPBA-4)2(OH)2·H2O]n, (TTPBA-4 = N,N,N′,N′-tetrakis(4-(4H-1,2,4-triazol-4-yl)phenyl)benzene-1,4-diamine), was developed and evaluated for its selectivity towards NACs [229]. 1D chains present in individual layers loped in perpendicular directions following warp-and-woof framework (Fig. 19 a) [231,232]. As compared to other NACs, TNP, having the lowest energy level in its LUMO, exhibited highest quenching with a Ksv of 4.7 × 104 M−1. Fig. shows the quenching in intensity with addition of TNP (Fig. 19b-c). However, LODs for DNP (1.4 ppm) and 4-NP (2.2 ppm) were also quite low as compared to other works. A one dimensional ladder like COP, [Cd(4-bpd)(3-cbn)2]n (4-bpd = 1,4-Bis(4-pyridyl)-2,3-diaza-1,3-butadiene, 3-cba = 3-chlorobenzoate) demonstrated a LOD of 0.260 μM towards TNP. The interesting architecture of the molecule was as a result of secondary interactions, namely H-bonding, Cl---π and C–H--- π there by leading to a supramolecular structure (Fig. 19d) [230]. Other Cd-based COPs have been previously reviewed to give remarkable results for HEMs sensing [233–236].

A one dimensional COP, [Zn(L)2(H2O)2] (L = 3-(3-pyridyl acrylate)) has been used to form a composite with fullerene, via electrostatic interaction between electron rich COP and electron deficient C60 [237]. Fig. 20 a and b shows the structure of the COP and the SEM image of the obtained composite. The COP based composite showcased a detection limit of 56 ppb for TNP. Jiang et al. reported that one dimensional chain of [Zn2(ctpy)2(bdc)]n and [Zn(ctpy)(suc)0.5]n (Hctpy = 2,2’:6′2″-terpyridine-4′-carboxylic acid, H2bdc = 1,4-benzene dicarboxylic acid, H2suc = succinic acid) underwent π-π stacking, individually to give rise to two sheet like COPs [238]. Fig. 20c shows the coordination environment of Zn in [Zn2(ctpy)2(bdc)]n. The COPs exhibited sensing ability towards phenolic NACs. The quenching by NACs followed the order TNP >2,4-DNP > 4-NP > 2-NP. [Zn2(CFDA)2(BPEP)]n·nDMF demonstrated a low detection limit of 43.93 ppb towards TNP due to the presence of guest molecule interaction sites on the backbone of the COP. The sensing process followed a combination of FRET, PET and inner filter effect [239].

Jing and co-workers synthesized a Zn complex, [Zn(L)2].(CH3OH)0.5⋅(H2O)2 (L = 3-chloro-6-(3-methyl-5-phenyl- 1H-pyrazol-1-yl) picolinic acid) [240]. The free uncoordinated carbonyl oxygen atoms on the surface coordinated with Ln3+ ions, namely, Tb and Eu (Fig. 21 a). The synthesized Ln complexes not only showed luminescence behaviour but also acted as naked-eye chemosensors for the detection of NACs. The quenching efficiency of various NACs with the said complex decreased from TNP to 4-NP, 2,6-DNT and was least for 4-NT. Tang et al. synthesized {[Zn2(bdtfa)(py)3(H2O)].2DMF}n at 80 °C in three days with tetrafluoro-bis(3,5- dicarboxyphenyl)terephthalamide (H4bdtfa) as the ligand [241]. The utilization of H4bdtfa was justified with regards to the presence of electronegative F atoms that acted as H-bond donors and acceptors and the electron rich conjugated backbone of the ligand, thereby aiding in the generation of supramolecular structures. Fig. 21b demonstrates the 2D sql-type structure of the COP. The 2-D layers could undergo π-π interactions and lead to a 3D supramolecular framework (Fig. 21c). Fig. 21d-e shows the quenching of fluorescence intensity of the COP on addition of NB and the corresponding Stern-Volmer plot. The luminescence was investigated preliminarily on a theoretical level, and the quenching inferred was attributed to energy and electron transfer.

Zn based COPs namely [Zn2(1,4-bdc)(1,4-Hbdc)2(NI-bpy-34)2] (1D ladder), [Zn2(2,6-ndc)(2,6-Hndc)2(NI-bpy-34)2]·H2O (2D thick layers) and [Zn(Hbtc)(NI-bpy-34)(H2O)]·H2O (2D grid), 1,4-H2bdc = benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid, 2,6-H2ndc = naphthalene-2,6-dicarboxylic acid, H3btc = benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid, NI-bpy-34 = N-(pyridin-3-yl)-4-(pyridin-4-yl)-1,8-naphthalimide, were synthesized via hydrothermal method [242]. The COPs exhibited dual luminescence, showing blue light emission, which was due to intra ligand transitions in NI-bpy-34. In another work, selective detection of 2,4-DNT was reported by the use of [Zn2(L)]n, {[Cd3(L)2(H2O)][Na2(μ2-H2O)(H2O)7]}n and [Zn2(L)(phen)2(H2O)·H2O]n (H4L = 5-[bis(3- carboxybenzyl)-amino]isophthalic acid, phen = 1,10-phenanthroline)via both energy and electron transfer processes [243]. Molla et al. developed moderately sensitive COPs, [Zn(H2O)2(L1)] and [Zn(L2)2]⋅0⋅.5H2O (L1 2– = 5-((4-carboxyphenyl)ethynyl)picolinate; L2 – = 5-(pyridin-3-ylethynyl)-picolinate) [244]. The photoluminescence of the synthesized compounds was confirmed by the absorption bands 215–465 nm and 202–400 nm, respectively, attributed to the intra ligand π → π ∗ and n → π ∗ transitions. Zn2+ being inert (d10 configuration), cannot undergo MLCT, thereby increasing the probability of ligand centered transitions [245]. It was observed that luminescence was pH dependent and declined in extremely acidic or basic conditions due to disarray of hydrogen bonded network or decomposition of Zn(II) complex [244]. Bhat and co-workers recently developed a coordination polymer using benzene tetra amine and lanthanide metal. The COP gave remarkable results in the detection of p-NP, TNT, TNP, RDX, CL-20, and PETN [4]. This was by far the only COP giving excellent results for a range of aromatic as well as non-aromatic explosives. It was reported to give a LOD of 1.5 μM for TNT. Fig. 21f-g shows the reduction in photoluminescence intensity and the Stern-Volmer plot for the same.

Several COPs have been employed for explosive sensing due to their ease of synthesis, structural diversity, and ability to fine-tune their photophysical properties through ligand modification. The quenching mechanism in these systems is predominantly static, facilitating the development of visual detection devices. However, challenges such as limited water stability and potential interference from other analytes remain. Future research should focus on enhancing the aqueous stability of COPs and improving their selectivity towards specific explosive compounds to broaden their practical applicability in real-world scenarios.

The development of functional materials for fluorescence-based detection of HEMs has progressed rapidly, driven by the need for sensitive, selective, and real-time sensing in security, environmental, and forensic contexts. This review has highlighted the strategic integration of diverse material classes, including molecular fluorophores, nanomaterials, polymer composites, COPs, MOFs, and COFs, each offering unique advantages in emission tuning and analyte-specific recognition via quenching mechanisms. Materials such as MOFs and COFs provide ordered porosity and tunable host–guest interactions, while nanocomposites and polymers afford flexibility, processability, and device compatibility. Advanced design strategies have enabled ultra-low detection limits and rapid response to explosive moieties. This review offers a unified perspective on converging principles across material platforms, moving beyond isolated categories to emphasize synergies in sensor design. By mapping cross-disciplinary strategies, it enables researchers to make informed choices in tailoring next-generation fluorometric sensors for practical deployment. Despite significant advances, key challenges remain in fluorescence-based detection of HEMs. Current sensors typically achieve detection limits in the nM to pM range, but real-world applications demand ppt-level sensitivity to match instrumental techniques. Most sensors are also limited to NACs, with poor detection of other classes such as non-NACs and inorganic nitrates, due to their weak electron-accepting nature and lack of specific binding sites. Performance in complex matrices is often compromised by interferents, photobleaching, and environmental instability. Additionally, scalability in material synthesis and device fabrication remains limited, and the absence of standardized performance metrics hinders reproducibility and validation.

To overcome current limitations and advance fluorescence-based explosive detection, future research should focus on the rational design of materials with tailored recognition sites, leveraging π–π interactions, hydrogen bonding, and supramolecular chemistry for enhanced selectivity. Hybrid platforms that integrate fluorescent nanomaterials with porous frameworks or responsive polymers can deliver synergistic effects, improving sensitivity, signal stability, and multi-analyte detection. Emphasis on miniaturization through flexible substrates, inkjet printing, and low-power electronics will enable the development of portable, field-deployable devices. Portability, coupled with real-time detection capabilities, is critical for enabling rapid, on-site analysis in security and environmental monitoring. The integration of AI and machine learning into fluorescence signal processing can significantly enhance real-time detection accuracy. Establishing standardized testing protocols is crucial for performance benchmarking and regulatory acceptance. Lastly, sustainability should guide future designs, prioritizing green synthesis, recyclable components, and biocompatible materials to ensure environmental safety and scalable deployment.

Samika Anand: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Kalathiparambil Rajendra Pai Sunajadevi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization, Validation. Channabasaveshwar V. Yelamaggad: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization.
No primary research results, software or code have been included and no new data were generated or analysed as part of this review.
